sentence: i šē aslan=ä bä xuš=i åmår-än
glosses_en: this shirt PN=OBL LOC delight=POSS:3 IPFV:come₂-PRS:3
comms_internal: CV. SS: “What remains unclear to me is whether and how this pattern can be seen as syntactically different from the regular transitive pattern?” MS: The oblique argument is embedded in the recipient, and the verb remains intransitive: “The shirt goes [to the delight [of Aslan]]”. The fact that bä can bisect the possessive construction does not transform aslan=ä into a “floating” argument of “to come”; it just shows that prepositions in Tat behave increasingly like case markers and are placed the closest possible to head nouns. This option is preferred because it is the closest to the Russian stimulus sentence. Alternatives are “aslan=ä äz i šē xuš=i åmår-än.” [PN=OBL from this shirt delight=POSS:3 IPFV:come₂-PRS:3] with the OBL:GEN_a pattern, and “aslan i šē=ä begän bäst-än.” [PN this shirt=OBL liking IPFV.do₂-PRS:3] with the TR pattern.

comms: Not included in the database because Y is expressed as an NP-internal modifier.
comms_internal: MS: “buy benzin” is an ezafe construction that has lost the ezafe morpheme and could, in theory, be reconstructed as *buy-i benzin ‘smell of gasoline’ (though the word benzin did not exist in Tat at the time the ezafe was lost). In Tat, there is more than one way to derive modifier-head constructions in which both constituents are nouns. One way is the inherited ezafe construction, as seen above. The other is the Turkic-influenced double-marked (OBL, POSS) construction. Theoretically, the noun phrase above could also be expressed as benzin=ä buy=i <gasoline=OBL smell=POSS:3>. The distribution of these two constructions varies by dialect, but modern Tat speakers typically reserve the ezafe construction for noun phrases expressing tight associations, e.g. when the head noun is a kinship term, when the modifier noun functions more as an attribute/qualifier than a possessor than, when both nouns are perceived as forming a single lexical unit, etc.

sentence: kälä bir-ä xune=yi aslan=ä xub bä yår=i mund-e
glosses_en: big be₂-PTCP house=POSS:3 PN=OBL good LOC memory=POSS:3 stay₂-PRF:3
back_translation_en: ‘Aslan remembers the house where he grew up well.’
comms_internal: Interesting! I understand the structure of this sentence, I think (lit. ‘The house... stays well in Aslan’s memory’) and if this is true, then we see one more example where the possessor-like nominal “aslan=ä” is linearly separated from the expected possessee (“memory”) implying that it is probably a clause-level dependent.

CV. SS. Do you think this pattern can be syntactically characterized as a transitive construction despite the fact that the verb is literally ‘to fall’? MS. Absolutely. This would also be the only case in Tat (that I can think of) where a complex predicate based on ‘to fall’ would be transitive.

comms_internal: This is similar to patterns involving EXT in some languages of the region. MS: “The oblique marking on the experiencer here is only possible because of the possessive-marked head noun later in the sentence and that this is different from genuine “oblique-subject” experiencer constructions, such as (2), which are marginal in Tat but do exist. You will find that in Abşeron Tat (see the other dataset I sent you two days ago), complex predicates with (originally) possessive clitics on the coverb sometimes lose the possessive clitic but maintain the oblique marker on what was originally the nominal modifier of the coverb, which yields, once again, a genuine “oblique-subject” experiencer construction.”

comms: The possessor NP displays some properties of a clause-level dependent.
comms_internal: CV. MS: “The syntactic subject here is čåy, and the sentence literally translates as ‘Tea comes to Aslan’s delight’. The splitting of the possessive construction by the subject is unusual but as I said in (1), such insertions are grammatical, even if pragmatically marked. In this case, the pragmatic motivation for the structure stems from the rhythm of the interview, as ironic as it sounds. Since I repeatedly asked my consultant to translate sentences featuring the same character, “Aslan” became topicalised and the focus shifted to “tea” as the more salient element of the sentence. This explains its focal position in the construction. If the sentence had been about somebody else other than Aslan, we may have ended up with something like čåy (another name)=ä bä xuš=i åmår-än.”
